codeSnapshot.label: vit_demo.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import torch
import timm  # pip install timm

# --- Load pretrained ViT-B/16 ---
model = timm.create_model('vit_base_patch16_224', pretrained=True)
model.eval()

params = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())
print(f"ViT-B/16 parameters: {params/1e6:.1f}M")  # ~86M

# --- Inspect patch embedding ---
print(f"Patch embed: {model.patch_embed}")
# PatchEmbed: kernel_size=16, stride=16, in_chans=3, embed_dim=768
# Number of patches: (224/16)^2 = 196

# --- Forward pass ---
x = torch.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)  # [B, C, H, W]
with torch.no_grad():
    features = model.forward_features(x)  # [1, 197, 768]  (196 patches + 1 CLS)
    logits   = model(x)                   # [1, 1000]

print(f"Feature shape (incl CLS): {features.shape}")  # [1, 197, 768]
print(f"Output shape:            {logits.shape}")     # [1, 1000]

# --- CLS token vs patch tokens ---
cls_token    = features[:, 0, :]     # [1, 768]  — used for classification
patch_tokens = features[:, 1:, :]    # [1, 196, 768]  — spatial features
print(f"CLS token shape:   {cls_token.shape}")
print(f"Patch token shape: {patch_tokens.shape}")

eli3: Before ViT, everyone assumed convolutional networks were the only way to process images. ViT challenged this by treating an image as a sequence of patches — just like how BERT treats a sentence as a sequence of words. Cut the image into 16×16 tiles, flatten each tile into a vector, and feed the sequence to a standard Transformer.

A special [CLS] token is prepended to the sequence (borrowed from BERT). After the Transformer encoder processes all patches (allowing each patch to attend to all others), the CLS token's final representation is used for classification — it acts as a summary of the whole image.

The catch: ViT needs much more data than CNNs to train from scratch. CNNs have translation equivariance baked in (the same filter slides over the whole image), but ViT has no such prior — it must learn spatial relationships from scratch, requiring large datasets (ImageNet-21k or JFT-300M). But once pretrained on enough data, ViT matches or beats CNNs on most vision tasks.

whenWhy.title: Large-scale image classification with large datasets
whenWhy.body: ViT matches CNN performance on ImageNet (1.2M images) when pretrained on larger datasets (ImageNet-21k: 14M images, or JFT-300M: 300M images). At scale (ViT-L/16 pretrained on JFT-300M), ViT achieves 88.5% top-1 on ImageNet — higher than EfficientNet-B7. The inductive biases of CNNs (local connectivity, weight sharing) are helpful for small datasets but become less important at scale when the model can learn these patterns from data. ViT is now the preferred architecture for large-scale vision pretraining.

whenWhy.title: Multimodal models (CLIP, GPT-4V, LLaVA)
whenWhy.body: ViT is the standard visual encoder in multimodal models. CLIP trains a ViT alongside a text Transformer using contrastive loss on 400M image-text pairs. GPT-4V, LLaVA, and Flamingo all use ViT as the image encoder. The advantage over CNNs: Transformers share the same architecture across modalities, simplifying cross-attention between visual and text tokens. The ViT's patch tokens (one per image patch) can be directly attended to by a language model decoder, enabling efficient visual question answering.

whenWhy.title: Dense prediction tasks with DINO and masked image modeling
whenWhy.body: ViT features learned via self-supervised objectives (DINO, DINOv2, MAE) provide excellent dense prediction representations. DINO ViT-S features naturally localise objects without any segmentation supervision — each patch token's representation reflects its semantic content. DINOv2 ViT-G features are the current state of the art for linear probing on depth estimation, segmentation, and retrieval without any fine-tuning. MAE (Masked Autoencoder, He et al., 2022) masks 75% of patches and trains ViT to reconstruct them — efficient pretraining that requires only a fraction of the patches for the encoder.

deepDiveIntro: Dosovitskiy et al. (2020) cut a 224-pixel image into a grid of non-overlapping 16-by-16-pixel patches, flattened each patch to a vector, and linearly projected it to the model dimension. A learned positional embedding is added to each patch token, and a learnable classification token is prepended. The resulting sequence of 197 tokens passes through twelve standard Transformer encoder layers. The final representation of the classification token is fed to a linear head for prediction. ViT-B/16 has 768 model dimensions, twelve attention heads, and 86 million parameters. [1]

ViT lacks the convolutional inductive biases of local receptive fields, weight sharing, and translation equivariance, so it must learn spatial structure entirely from data. Trained from scratch on ImageNet-1k with 1.2 million images it performs comparably to but not better than ResNet. Performance improves substantially with pretraining on larger datasets, self-supervised objectives such as DINO or masked autoencoding, or distillation from a CNN teacher as in DeiT. At scale or with strong pretraining, ViT matches or exceeds CNN performance on most vision tasks. [2]

deepDive.heading: Patch Embedding and Input Preparation
deepDive.body: The patch embedding is implemented as a single strided convolution with kernel and stride equal to the patch size, which extracts all patches and projects them to the model dimension in one efficient operation. A learned positional embedding vector is then added to each patch token. The classification token is a single learned vector prepended to the sequence before the encoder. This minimal input preparation means the Transformer backbone itself is identical to the language model version. [1]

deepDive.heading: ViT vs CNN: Inductive Biases and Data Efficiency
deepDive.body: CNNs encode two strong priors about images: local receptive fields capture nearby pixel relationships, and weight sharing across positions enforces translation equivariance. These priors make CNNs very efficient on limited data. ViT has no such priors — every patch attends to every other patch from the first layer, giving it the flexibility to learn any spatial relationship but requiring far more data to discover the structure that CNNs receive for free. At sufficient scale or with strong pretraining, ViT surpasses CNNs because it is not constrained by the locality assumption, which can be limiting for complex global reasoning tasks. [2]

deepDive.heading: Self-Supervised ViT: DINO and MAE
deepDive.body: DINO trains a student ViT to match the output of an exponentially averaged teacher on different crops of the same image, without any labels. A key empirical finding is that DINO attention heads naturally segment objects in their last layer without any supervision. MAE masks 75% of patches at random and trains the encoder on only the visible patches to reconstruct the missing ones. The high masking ratio works because adjacent patches are highly redundant; learning to reconstruct masked regions forces the model to develop semantic understanding. MAE pretraining is about three times faster than contrastive methods and achieves strong fine-tuning performance. [2]

deepDive.heading: ViT Scaling: Variants and Parameter Counts
deepDive.body: Accuracy vs efficiency: B/16 (86M, 17.6 GFLOPs) achieves 81.8% on ImageNet after fine-tuning from ImageNet-21k; L/16 (307M) achieves 85.2%. DINOv2 ViT-G/14 (1B params) achieves 86.5% without any supervised fine-tuning on ImageNet — just linear probing.

takeaways: ViT splits image into P×P patches, flattens, linearly projects, prepends [CLS] token, adds positional embeddings, passes to Transformer encoder. [1]
ViT-B/16: P=16, D=768, L=12 layers, 12 heads; 196 patches from 224×224 image + 1 CLS = 197 tokens. [1]
No CNN inductive biases: ViT needs large datasets (ImageNet-21k or self-supervised) but models arbitrary long-range dependencies. [2]
DINO ViT attention heads naturally segment foreground objects without supervision — emergent spatial structure. [2]
MAE masks 75% of patches; reconstruction pretraining is 3× faster than contrastive methods and matches downstream accuracy. [2]

edgeCases.q: Why does ViT use a [CLS] token for classification instead of average-pooling patch tokens?
edgeCases.a: Both approaches work in practice. The [CLS] token approach (from BERT) was used in the original ViT: the [CLS] token has no spatial meaning and is free to aggregate global information from all patch tokens via self-attention during training. Average pooling (GlobalAveragePool over all patch tokens) is conceptually simpler and was found to work equally well or slightly better in some ViT ablations. DeiT and many follow-up works use average pooling as the default. The [CLS] token has a practical advantage: it provides a fixed-size, position-independent global representation that's easy to use for retrieval and downstream tasks without knowing the sequence length. For dense prediction (segmentation, depth), patch tokens are used directly — the [CLS] token is discarded.

edgeCases.q: How do you fine-tune a ViT pretrained on 224×224 images on a higher-resolution task?
edgeCases.a: ViT's positional embeddings are learned 1D vectors indexed by position — 196 position embeddings for 196 patches at 224×224. At 448×448 with P=16, you get 784 patches — more positions than the pretrained model has. Solution: 2D bicubic interpolation of the pretrained position embeddings from a 14×14 grid (196 positions) to a 28×28 grid (784 positions). PyTorch's interpolate function handles this: reshape the 196 embeddings to (14,14), interpolate to (28,28), flatten back to (784, D). This allows the model to generalise to unseen resolutions. The patch embedding (Conv2d weights) is reused directly — patch size P stays the same. Fine-tuning at higher resolution with interpolated PE typically gives 1–2% accuracy improvement over using the original 224×224 resolution.

sources.label: Dosovitskiy et al. (2020) — An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale, ICLR 2021 arXiv:2010.11929
He et al. (2022) — Masked Autoencoders Are Scalable Vision Learners, CVPR 2022 arXiv:2111.06377
